---
title: "Solvers and numerical methods in geometry"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
Almost everything in this unit bottoms out in **three** numerical workhorses, and
none of them touches a dense factorisation:

- a **matrix-free conjugate-gradient** solve ([`cg`](#sec-cg)) for the
  symmetric-positive-definite heat-smoothing system,
- a **shift-invert / spectral-folding eigensolver**
  ([`eigsolve_top_k`](#sec-eigensolve)) for the smallest Laplace–Beltrami
  eigenpairs (manifold harmonics, spectral embedding),
- the **semiring gather-matmul** ([`semiring_ell_matmul` and friends](#sec-kernels))
  that *is* every local operator — diffusion, morphology, the distance transform,
  every mesh apply — and carries the differentiability story for the whole unit.

All three are GPU-native and **cuSolver-free**. The dense, factorisation-heavy
machinery that powers the [stats unit's solvers](../statistics/solvers.qmd) — damped Newton,
Woodbury, the FaST-LMM spectral trick — mostly *does not appear here*: geometry
poses different problems. This page says what is used, why, and what is
deliberately absent. It also tours the load-bearing **host-side construction**
routines (marching tetrahedra, the broad-phase spatial index, the icosphere
builder, the priority-flood watershed) and the one rule that governs them: *never
call a host op inside a jitted loop*.
:::

This is the reference-tier page for numerical methods. The geometric *meaning* of
the operators lives elsewhere — the [cotangent Laplacian](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent),
the [manifold harmonics](spectral.qmd#sec-eigen), the
[ELL storage format](operators.qmd#sec-ell) — and is assumed here. What follows is
the layer underneath: the arithmetic that makes those operators *run*, *converge*,
and *differentiate*.

## A motivating problem: smooth a thickness map along the cortex {#sec-motivation}

Here is a concrete neuroimaging task that exercises every solver on this page.
You have a cortical surface mesh $(V, F)$ — call it 150 000 vertices — and a
per-vertex scalar: cortical thickness, or a myelin estimate, or a first-level GLM
$t$-statistic. You want to **smooth it along the surface**, not through the
ambient space, so that two banks of a sulcus that happen to be 2 mm apart in $\mathbb{R}^3$
but 30 mm apart *across the cortical sheet* do not bleed into each other.

The right tool is **heat diffusion on the manifold**. Diffuse the field for a
short time $t$ and you get a Gaussian smoother whose notion of distance is
*geodesic*. Solving that diffusion stably for a finite step means solving one
symmetric-positive-definite linear system — and that is a job for conjugate
gradients ([@sec-cg]). If instead you want the *natural modes* of the surface —
the manifold harmonics that a spectral embedding or a surface registration is
built on — you want the smallest eigenpairs of the same Laplacian, and that is a
job for the shift-invert eigensolver ([@sec-eigensolve]). And underneath both, the
matrix–vector product $L\mathbf{x}$ that they call thousands of times is a single
templated gather-and-reduce ([@sec-kernels]). We will follow this one example down
through all three layers.

The discipline that ties the page together is the unit's governing idea: the mesh
is a **discrete approximation of a smooth surface**. A good solver is one whose
answer *converges to the continuous truth as the mesh is refined*, and that we can
*validate* against an analytic oracle — a sphere whose harmonics we know in closed
form, a heat kernel whose total mass is conserved exactly. We check **class and
correlation**, never bit-for-bit equality, because the discrete operator is only
ever an approximation of the smooth one it stands in for.

## The semiring kernels: one gather, many operators {#sec-kernels}

Start with the most-used "solver" of all — though it is not a solver in the
linear-algebra sense, it is the primitive every other routine calls. Almost every
local geometric operation has the same shape: **for each element, look at its
neighbours, combine each neighbour's value with an edge weight, then reduce the
combined values to a single number.** A graph diffusion does it with multiply-and-add.
A morphological dilation does it with add-and-max. A one-step shortest-path
relaxation does it with add-and-min. The Euclidean distance transform does it with
squared-difference-and-min. These look like four different algorithms, but they
are *one* algorithm with two pluggable operations.

### The idea, then the formula

Name the two operations. Let $\otimes$ be the **combine** (how an edge weight
acts on a neighbour value) and $\oplus$ be the **reduce** (how the combined
contributions are accumulated). A pair $(\oplus, \otimes)$ with the right
algebraic laws — associativity, an identity for each, $\otimes$ distributing over
$\oplus$ — is a **semiring**. Once you have that vocabulary, the whole family of
local operators collapses into a single contraction. For a sparse operator $A$
stored row-by-row and a dense right operand $B$:

$$
C[i, j] = \bigoplus_{p}\; \Big( \texttt{values}[i, p] \;\otimes\; B[\,\texttt{indices}[i, p],\, j\,] \Big)
$$ {#eq-semiring-ell}

Read it left to right: row $i$ has a neighbour list $\texttt{indices}[i, :]$ of
length $k_{\max}$ with weights $\texttt{values}[i, :]$; for each neighbour $p$ we
**gather** the neighbour's row $B[\texttt{indices}[i,p], :]$, **combine** it with
the weight via $\otimes$, and **reduce** across the $k_{\max}$ neighbours via
$\oplus$. Choosing $(\oplus, \otimes) = (+, \times)$ recovers an ordinary
sparse matrix–vector product; choosing $(\max, +)$ gives a max-plus relaxation;
$(\min, +)$ gives min-plus. @fig-solvers-semiring shows the same gather producing
three different operators by the swap alone.

This is the ELL storage format from the [operators page](operators.qmd#sec-ell):
`values` and `indices` are both $(m, k_{\max})$, padded so that every row has the
same width. Padding positions point at any valid row of $B$ and carry the
**semiring identity** in `values` (e.g. $0$ for REAL, $-\infty$ for max-plus), so
the padded contribution is a no-op under $\oplus$.

### The reference kernel: a `fori_loop` over neighbours

The correctness floor for the whole library is the pure-JAX reference in
`semiring/_reference.py`. `reference_semiring_ell_matmul` walks the neighbour axis
with `lax.fori_loop`, and at step $p$ it slices column $p$ of `values` and
`indices`, gathers `B[idx_p]`, combines, and folds into an accumulator with the
semiring's `monoid.update`:

```python
def body(p, acc):
    v_p = values[:, p:p+1]            # (m, 1)
    idx_p = indices[:, p]             # (m,)
    gathered = B[idx_p]              # (m, ncol)
    value = binary_op.combine(v_p, gathered)
    return monoid.update(acc, value)
acc = lax.fori_loop(0, kmax, body, monoid.init((m, ncol), B.dtype))
return monoid.finalize(acc)
```

Two things matter here. First, the loop streams over neighbours so the
intermediate stays $(m, n_{\text{cols}})$ — it never materialises the $(m, k_{\max}, n_{\text{cols}})$
tensor of all gathered contributions. Second, the *same* pytree-state pattern
(`init` → `update` → `finalize`) is what the Pallas GPU kernel uses, so the
reference and the accelerated kernel exercise the algebra identically. The
backend-parity tests pin every Pallas kernel against this reference; if a Pallas
kernel cannot tile a given shape × algebra it falls back to the reference with a
`NitrixBackendFallback` warning. This is the validation posture again: not
bit-parity to a foreign tool, but *class agreement* between two of our own paths.

**Public surface.** The user-facing kernels (`DIFF-JAX` — jittable and
differentiable) are:

- `semiring_ell_matmul(values, indices, B, *, semiring=REAL, n_cols=None, backend='auto')`
  — @eq-semiring-ell, the gather-matmul. This is what a mesh `apply_operator`
  call resolves to.
- `semiring_ell_rmatvec(values, indices, X, *, semiring=REAL, n_cols)` — the
  **adjoint**: where the matmul gathers, this *scatters*,
  $Y[c, j] = \sum_{(i,p):\,\texttt{indices}[i,p]=c} \texttt{values}[i,p]\, X[i, j]$.
  It is **REAL-only** on purpose — the additive scatter needs linear structure,
  and a non-zero pad identity would scatter spurious mass, so any other algebra
  raises `NotImplementedError`. Composing the two gives the symmetric-part matvec
  $\tfrac12(AX + A^\top X) = \operatorname{sym}(A)X$ that the eigensolvers need when
  an adjacency's construction did not preserve symmetry (more in [@sec-eigensolve]).
- `semiring_matmul(A, B, *, semiring=REAL, ...)` — the dense semiring product,
  $C[i,j] = \bigoplus_k (A[i,k] \otimes B[k,j])$, also `fori_loop`-streamed over $k$.
- `semiring_conv(x, k, *, semiring=REAL, stride=1, padding='SAME', dilation=1, ...)`
  — the channels-last semiring convolution; it lowers to patches and then calls
  the dense `semiring_matmul` so it inherits the same per-algebra backward.

The built-in algebras (`semiring/algebras.py`) are `REAL` $(+,\times)$,
`LOG` $(\text{logsumexp}, +)$, `TROPICAL_MAX_PLUS` $(\max, +)$,
`TROPICAL_MIN_PLUS` $(\min, +)$, `EUCLIDEAN` (squared-difference under a
$\sqrt{\,}$ finalize), and `BOOLEAN` (OR/AND).

### The differentiability story: per-algebra `custom_vjp`

Gradients are where the semiring abstraction earns its keep. You cannot let JAX
autodiff blindly through the `fori_loop` — for a `max`-reduction that would be
wrong (the gradient of a max is not a smooth thing), and for the streaming loops
it would unroll an expensive tape. Instead each public kernel carries a
`jax.custom_vjp` whose backward dispatches to a **hand-derived rule per algebra**,
in `semiring/_backward.py`. The contract bar: *state the gradient as an idea,
then read the formula off it.*

- **REAL** is linear, so its backward is exactly the two transpose-contractions you
  would write for an ordinary matmul — nothing more is needed, because the forward
  is itself linear in both arguments. For the ELL form,
  $\partial L/\partial\texttt{values}[i,p] = \sum_j g_{\text{out}}[i,j]\, B[\texttt{indices}[i,p], j]$
  (a gather-dot), and the gradient w.r.t. $B$ is exactly the additive adjoint
  $A^\top g_{\text{out}}$ — implemented by *calling* `reference_semiring_ell_rmatvec`,
  so the rmatvec is the single source of truth for both the gradient and the
  symmetric matvec (`real_ell_matmul_vjp`).
- **LOG** weights each contribution by a softmax,
  $w[i,p,j] = \exp(\texttt{values}[i,p] + B[\texttt{indices}[i,p],j] - C[i,j])$,
  bounded in $[0,1]$ because $C$ is the logsumexp; the backward streams over
  neighbours so intermediates stay $(m, n)$, and $-\infty$ rows propagate cleanly
  through a guarded `exp`.
- **TROPICAL_MAX_PLUS / MIN_PLUS** are not differentiable at ties, so the
  backward is a **subgradient**: route the entire upstream gradient through the
  one-hot $[p = \arg\max_p(\ldots)]$. The code finds the winning neighbour with a
  streaming `fori_loop` (`_tropical_argk` for the dense form,
  `tropical_max_plus_ell_matmul_vjp` for ELL) and breaks ties toward the *first*
  maximiser, matching `jnp.argmax`. This is the right notion of derivative for a
  shortest-path or a dilation: an infinitesimal change to the field moves the
  output only through the edge that is currently winning.
- **EUCLIDEAN** has a $1/C$ factor (the derivative of $\sqrt{\,}$) that blows up
  at zero distance; `_safe_div` uses the "double-where with sentinel" trick to
  return a clean $0$ there instead of a `NaN`.
- **BOOLEAN** is not differentiable and its backward *raises* — a deliberate
  type error rather than a silent zero.

Two implementation details are load-bearing and easy to miss. First, **`indices`
cannot ride in `nondiff_argnums`**: JAX ≥ 0.10 refuses tracers there, so `indices`
is carried as an ordinary array argument and the backward returns a *zero gradient
of matching shape* for it — consistent with "integer indices are
non-differentiable". (The dense `semiring_matmul` *can* and does keep its static
args in `nondiff_argnums`.) Second, this is why morphology, the EDT, and the mesh
operators all inherit gradients **for free**: they are written as semiring calls,
so the moment you pick a differentiable algebra you get the hand-derived VJP with
no extra work. The geometry suite's entire backward story is *this* table.

```{python}
#| label: fig-solvers-semiring
#| fig-cap: "One gather, three operators. The same ELL ring-neighbour structure, reduced under three semirings: REAL (+, ×) performs one diffusion step on an impulse; MIN-PLUS (min, +) performs one shortest-path (Bellman–Ford) relaxation of a seed distance; MAX-PLUS (max, +) performs one bottleneck relaxation. Only the (⊕, ⊗) pair changes — the neighbour gather is identical."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

n = 60
i = np.arange(n)

# ELL of a ring: each row i has neighbours {i-1, i, i+1} (k_max = 3).
indices = np.stack([(i - 1) % n, i, (i + 1) % n], axis=1)
w_real = np.full((n, 3), 1.0 / 3.0)               # 3-tap smoother
edge = 1.0 + 0.5 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * i / n)      # tropical edge "lengths"
w_trop = np.stack([edge[(i - 1) % n], np.zeros(n), edge], axis=1)

def ell_reduce(values, indices, x, combine, reduce_init, reduce_op):
    out = np.full(n, reduce_init)
    for p in range(indices.shape[1]):
        gathered = x[indices[:, p]]
        out = reduce_op(out, combine(values[:, p], gathered))
    return out

real = ell_reduce(w_real, indices, np.where(i == n // 4, 1.0, 0.0),
                  combine=lambda v, g: v * g, reduce_init=0.0, reduce_op=np.add)
minp = ell_reduce(w_trop, indices, np.where(i == n // 4, 0.0, np.inf),
                  combine=lambda v, g: v + g, reduce_init=np.inf, reduce_op=np.minimum)
maxp = ell_reduce(w_trop, indices, np.where(i == n // 4, 0.0, -np.inf),
                  combine=lambda v, g: v + g, reduce_init=-np.inf, reduce_op=np.maximum)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13, 3.8), sharex=True)
axes[0].stem(i, real, basefmt=" ", linefmt="C0-", markerfmt="C0o")
axes[0].set_title(r"REAL $(+,\times)$" "\none diffusion step")
axes[1].stem(i, minp, basefmt=" ", linefmt="C2-", markerfmt="C2o")
axes[1].set_title(r"MIN-PLUS $(\min,+)$" "\none shortest-path relax")
axes[2].stem(i, maxp, basefmt=" ", linefmt="C3-", markerfmt="C3o")
axes[2].set_title(r"MAX-PLUS $(\max,+)$" "\none bottleneck relax")
for ax in axes:
    ax.set_xlabel("vertex on the ring")
axes[0].set_ylabel("output")
fig.suptitle("Swap the semiring, keep the gather: one kernel, three operators", y=1.03)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Matrix-free conjugate gradients {#sec-cg}

Now the heat smoother from [@sec-motivation]. Diffusing a field $\mathbf{x}_0$ for
time $t$ on the surface means evolving $\partial_\tau \mathbf{x} = -\Delta\mathbf{x}$,
where $\Delta = M^{-1}L$ is the [Laplace–Beltrami operator](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent):
$M$ the lumped [mass](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-mass) (vertex areas $A_i$ on the
diagonal) and $L$ the cotangent stiffness. A single **backward-Euler** step — the
stable, unconditionally-convergent implicit scheme — turns that PDE into a linear
solve.

### From the diffusion equation to an SPD system

The obvious thing is to step forward explicitly: $\mathbf{x}_{n+1} = \mathbf{x}_n - t\,\Delta\mathbf{x}_n$.
That fails when $t$ is anything but tiny — the explicit scheme is only stable for
$t$ below a mesh-dependent threshold, so a real smoothing radius would need
hundreds of micro-steps. The cure is one *backward* step, which evaluates the
Laplacian at the *new* time:
$\mathbf{x}_{n+1} = \mathbf{x}_n - t\,\Delta\mathbf{x}_{n+1}$. Rearranging, and
multiplying through by $M$ to keep the operator symmetric:

$$
(M + t\,L)\,\mathbf{x} = M\,\mathbf{x}_0,
\qquad t = \frac{\text{FWHM}^2}{16\ln 2}.
$$ {#eq-backward-euler}

The diffusion time $t$ is tied to the target full-width-at-half-maximum through
$\sigma^2 = 2t$ and $\text{FWHM} = 2\sqrt{2\ln 2}\,\sigma$, exactly as in
`surface_smooth`. The matrix on the left is **symmetric positive-definite**: $M$
is a positive diagonal, $L$ is PSD (it is a [cotangent Laplacian](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent)),
and $t > 0$. SPD is the magic word — it unlocks the cheapest iterative solver
there is.

### Why conjugate gradients, in one paragraph

For an SPD system $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$, solving the system is *identical* to
minimising the convex quadratic $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac12\mathbf{x}^\top A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^\top\mathbf{x}$,
whose gradient is the residual $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{b} - A\mathbf{x}$. The naive
minimiser is steepest descent — step along $-\nabla f = \mathbf{r}$ — but
successive steepest steps zig-zag and re-undo each other. **Conjugate gradients**
fixes the zig-zag with one idea: choose search directions that are
**$A$-conjugate** ($\mathbf{p}_i^\top A\,\mathbf{p}_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$), so that
minimising along a new direction never spoils the progress made along the old
ones. Each direction is "used up" exactly once, so in exact arithmetic CG reaches
the true solution in at most $n$ steps — and, crucially, in *far* fewer when the
eigenvalues of $A$ are *clustered*, which the heat operator $M + tL$ is. Every step
costs only **one matrix–vector product and a couple of inner products** — no
factorisation, ever. @fig-solvers-cg shows the residual dropping below tolerance in
a handful of steps and the intermediate iterates becoming visibly better smoothed
fields.

### How it is wired here

`nitrix.linalg.krylov.cg` (`DIFF-JAX`) is a thin, purposeful wrapper around
`jax.scipy.sparse.linalg.cg`:

```python
def cg(a, b, *, x0=None, tol=1e-6, atol=0.0, maxiter=None, l2=0.0):
    op = a if callable(a) else (lambda v: a @ v)
    def matvec(v):
        return op(v) + l2 * v
    x, _ = _jax_cg(matvec, b, x0=x0, tol=tol, atol=atol, maxiter=maxiter)
    return x
```

Three design choices are worth naming. First, `a` may be a **callable** — the
matrix is never materialised. `surface_smooth` passes a closure that applies the
operator through the [apply seam](operators.qmd#sec-apply):

```python
def _matvec(v):
    lv = apply_operator(lap, v[..., None])[..., 0]   # L @ v via the ELL kernel
    return area * v + t * lv                          # (M + tL) v
return cg(_matvec, area * values, tol=tol, maxiter=maxiter)
```

So the heat solve is *built on the semiring kernel of [@sec-kernels]*: every CG
iteration's matvec is a `semiring_ell_matmul` under REAL. Second, the optional
`l2` ridge adds $\lambda\mathbf{v}$ to the matvec unconditionally (a no-op at
$\lambda = 0$) and may be a *traced* scalar, so a Levenberg–Marquardt damping can
vary inside a `jit`. Third — and this is why CG, not a Cholesky — it is
**cuSolver-free**: built from matvecs and dot products only, it never touches the
dense-solver handle pool that wedges on the dev L4 GPU (see [@sec-host] and the
[`_solver` discussion](#sec-not-used)). It is the device-resilient SPD path.

### Gradients through the solve, for free

`surface_smooth` is differentiable w.r.t. the field `values`, and there is no
unrolling of the CG loop to make that so. `jax.scipy.sparse.linalg.cg` carries its
own **implicit-differentiation rule**: the gradient of the solution of $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$
satisfies the *adjoint system* $A^\top \boldsymbol\lambda = \mathbf{g}$, which (for
symmetric $A$) is another CG solve. So the backward pass is one more matrix-free CG,
not a tape of every forward iteration — constant memory in the iteration count.

### A genuine cross-unit shared solver

This is the *same* `cg` the [stats unit's solvers page](../statistics/solvers.qmd) reaches
for. There, the Gauss–Newton / Levenberg–Marquardt normal equations
$(J^\top J + \lambda\,\text{diag})\,\boldsymbol\delta = -J^\top\mathbf{r}$ of the
registration path are SPD and small, and solving them matrix-free on-device beats a
host round-trip to a CPU Cholesky. Geometry's heat smoother and stats' LM step are
*different problems that share one numerical kernel* — the clearest case on this
page of a solver that recurs across both units. The contrast with everything in
[@sec-not-used] is the point: this one crosses over; most do not.

```{python}
#| label: fig-solvers-cg
#| fig-cap: "Conjugate gradients on the backward-Euler heat system (M + tL)x = Mx₀, here on an analytic ring Laplacian (n = 128). Left: the relative residual falls below tol = 1e-6 in far fewer than n steps because the heat operator's spectrum is clustered. Right: the CG iterates rapidly approach the exact smoothed field — each iterate is itself a usable, progressively smoother solution."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# Analytic SPD heat system on a ring: L is the discrete -d^2/dx^2, M = I.
n = 128
t = 8.0
i = np.arange(n)
L = 2.0 * np.eye(n) - np.eye(n, k=1) - np.eye(n, k=-1)
L[0, -1] = -1.0
L[-1, 0] = -1.0
A = np.eye(n) + t * L               # (M + tL), SPD

x_true = np.exp(-0.5 * ((i - n / 2) / 6.0) ** 2)
b = x_true + 0.05 * rng.standard_normal(n)

def cg(matvec, b, n_iter):
    x = np.zeros_like(b)
    r = b - matvec(x)
    p = r.copy()
    rs = r @ r
    hist = [np.sqrt(rs) / np.linalg.norm(b)]
    iters = [x.copy()]
    for _ in range(n_iter):
        Ap = matvec(p)
        alpha = rs / (p @ Ap)          # exact line-search along the A-conjugate p
        x = x + alpha * p
        r = r - alpha * Ap
        rs_new = r @ r
        p = r + (rs_new / rs) * p       # new direction, A-conjugate to the last
        rs = rs_new
        hist.append(np.sqrt(rs) / np.linalg.norm(b))
        iters.append(x.copy())
    return x, np.array(hist), iters

_, hist, iterates = cg(lambda v: A @ v, b, 40)
x_exact = np.linalg.solve(A, b)

fig, (ax0, ax1) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4.2))
ax0.semilogy(hist, color="C3", marker="o", ms=3, lw=1.2)
ax0.axhline(1e-6, color="0.5", ls="--", lw=1, label="tol = 1e-6")
ax0.set_xlabel("CG iteration")
ax0.set_ylabel(r"relative residual $\|b - Ax\|/\|b\|$")
ax0.set_title("CG converges in $\\ll n$ steps")
ax0.legend(frameon=False)

ax1.plot(i, b, color="0.7", lw=1, label="noisy input $x_0$")
for k, c in zip((1, 3, 8), ("C0", "C1", "C2")):
    ax1.plot(i, iterates[k], lw=1.2, color=c, label=f"CG iterate {k}")
ax1.plot(i, x_exact, "k--", lw=1.4, label="exact solve")
ax1.set_xlabel("vertex index on the ring")
ax1.set_ylabel("field value")
ax1.set_title("Each iterate is a better heat-smoothed field")
ax1.legend(frameon=False, fontsize=8)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Eigensolving the Laplace–Beltrami operator {#sec-eigensolve}

The other half of the motivating problem: the **manifold harmonics**. The
eigenfunctions $\phi_k$ of the Laplace–Beltrami operator, solving the generalised
problem $L\phi_k = \lambda_k M\phi_k$, are the surface's analogue of Fourier modes
— and the [spectral page](spectral.qmd#sec-eigen) builds [heat kernels](spectral.qmd#sec-heat-kernel)
and [embeddings](spectral.qmd#sec-embedding) on them. We want the **smallest**
eigenpairs: $\lambda_0 = 0$ (the constant mode), then the lowest-frequency,
smoothest, most global modes. This page is the *numerical sibling* of that material
— spectral [explains what the modes mean](spectral.qmd#sec-eigen); here we explain
how to *compute* them, and link rather than duplicate.

### The obvious approach, and exactly where it breaks

The obvious approach: run an iterative eigensolver. **LOBPCG** (Locally Optimal
Block Preconditioned Conjugate Gradient) is the natural choice — it is to the
eigenvalue problem what CG is to the linear one. Its core idea is the **Rayleigh
quotient**: for a symmetric operator $A$,

$$
\rho(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\mathbf{x}^\top A\, \mathbf{x}}{\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{x}},
$$ {#eq-rayleigh}

is minimised by the smallest eigenvector and *maximised* by the largest, with the
eigenvalue as the optimal value. LOBPCG maximises $\rho$ over a *block* of vectors
at once (so you get $k$ pairs together), and at each step it searches the subspace
spanned by the current block, its $A$-image, and the previous block — a
CG-flavoured three-term recurrence that uses only matvecs. Pure matvecs, no
factorisation: exactly the device-resilient discipline we want.

But here is where it breaks, and the brief names the measured failure: iterative
eigensolvers find the **largest** eigenvalues robustly, and they resolve eigenpairs
in proportion to the **relative gap** between neighbouring eigenvalues. The
smallest LBO eigenvalues are the worst possible case on *both* counts — they are at
the *wrong end*, and they **cluster near zero** (a closed surface has
$\lambda_0 = 0$ and a tight low cluster above it). The team's own measurement on a
spherical-parameterisation surface: *a plain reflection + LOBPCG folds ~47% of
triangles* — nearly half the mesh inverts — because the near-zero modes are not
resolved cleanly enough to recover the constant-plus-first-three subspace the
embedding needs (`docs/design/geometry-suite.md`).

### Two fixes, stacked: fold, then shift-invert

The cure is two ideas, each motivated by the failure it removes.

**Fix 1 — spectral folding, to put the smallest at the top.** Iterative methods
want the largest, so *make the smallest the largest* with a monotone map that
reverses order. The connectopy/geometry path symmetrises the operator to
$\tilde L = M^{-1/2}LM^{-1/2}$ and forms the **affinity**

$$
A = I - \tilde L / c,
$$ {#eq-fold}

with $c$ a spectral-scale constant. Since $\tilde L \succeq 0$, $A$'s eigenvalues
are $1 - \lambda_k/c$ — *largest where $\lambda_k$ is smallest*. Now a top-$k$
*largest* solve on $A$ returns the *smallest* harmonics of $L$. This is exactly the
`L = I - M` shift the code hard-codes. But folding alone does not change the
*gaps*: a tiny absolute gap near $0$ becomes a tiny *relative* gap at the top of a
wide spectrum, which is still hard (@fig-solvers-spectral, middle panel — the
near-0 cluster stays cramped).

**Fix 2 — shift-invert, to blow the near-zero gaps open.** The decisive move is to
solve eigenpairs of $(L - \sigma I)^{-1}$ instead of $L$. This operator shares
$L$'s eigenvectors but has eigenvalues $1/(\lambda_k - \sigma)$, which **explode
for $\lambda_k$ near the shift $\sigma$**. Put $\sigma$ just below the smallest
eigenvalue (the code's `SolverSpec.shift_invert(sigma=...)` defaults $\sigma = -0.5$;
the geometry path uses $\sigma = -0.01$, just below $\lambda_0 = 0$) and the
clustered near-zero modes are mapped to enormous, *well-separated* values. The
relative gaps the solver actually sees grow by roughly two orders of magnitude
(@fig-solvers-spectral, right panel, and its caption number). You never form the
inverse: each application of $(L - \sigma I)^{-1}$ is an **inner CG solve** against
the shifted operator, which is SPD for $\sigma < 0$. So shift-invert is *LOBPCG on
the outside, CG on the inside* — both matrix-free, both cuSolver-free, the whole
edifice built from the matvecs of [@sec-kernels].

In code (`linalg/_eigsolve.py`, `_run_shift_invert_method`):

```python
def shifted(y):
    return (1.0 - sigma) * y - apply(y)        # (1 - sigma) I - A, SPD for sigma < 0
def si_op(x):
    sol, _ = cg(shifted, x, maxiter=cg_iters)   # inner CG: apply the shifted inverse
    return sol
mu, eigvecs, _ = lobpcg_standard(si_op, X0, m=outer_iters)
eigvals = 1.0 - sigma - 1.0 / mu                # un-shift back to the M-eigenvalues
```

### The dispatcher: one forward per method, one backward per format

`eigsolve_top_k(operand, k, *, spec=SolverSpec.auto(), seed=0)` (`DIFF-JAX`,
returning an `EigPair(values, vectors)` largest-first) is the user-facing entry
point, and its architecture is the elegant part. The key factoring: **the solver
forward is orthogonal to the gradient backward.** The forward depends on the
*method* (which iteration) and is written against an abstract matvec
`apply: X -> M @ X`, so it is format-independent. The backward depends only on the
*converged eigenpair* (via the implicit-VJP), so it is method-independent and
specialised only by operand *format*.

- **`SolverSpec`** is a frozen, hashable dataclass carrying all static config
  (`method`, `n_iters`, `tol`, `sigma`, `outer_iters`, `cg_iters`, `degree`,
  `shift`, `eps_clamp`, `promise_symmetry`). Hashability is load-bearing: the whole
  spec rides the `custom_vjp` `nondiff_argnums` as one argument, so the forward
  branches on `spec.method` at *trace* time. Build it with the classmethods —
  `SolverSpec.auto()`, `.eigh()`, `.lobpcg(...)`, `.shift_invert(...)`, `.poly(...)`.
- **The methods.** `eigh` (dense-only) is a full `safe_eigh` sliced to the top-$k$
  — folded in only in its *extremal* role, it does not subsume `safe_eigh`'s
  full-spectrum uses. `lobpcg` is plain LOBPCG. `shift_invert` is the fix above.
  `poly` applies a shifted-power spectral filter $(M + \text{shift}\,I)^{\text{degree}}$
  to amplify the wanted end, recovering eigenvalues by the Rayleigh quotient
  (@eq-rayleigh). `auto` resolves to `eigh` for dense input and `lobpcg` for sparse
  — a static, format-based choice with no tracer inspection. The matvec-only
  methods (`lobpcg`, `shift_invert`, `poly`) serve all three operand formats
  (dense, `ELL`, `SectionedELL`); `eigh` is dense-only.
- **The shared backward.** All formats compute the same $k\times k$ core
  $K = \operatorname{diag}(\mathbf{g}_\lambda) + S \odot F$ (`_subspace_vjp_kernel`),
  where $F[i,j] = 1/(\lambda_j - \lambda_i)$ — *clamped to zero where eigenvalues
  are within `eps_clamp`*, the standard guard against the singularity of degenerate
  eigenvalues — and $S$ is the antisymmetric part of the projected eigenvector
  cotangent. Then a per-format projection: `_project_dense` returns
  $dM = VKV^\top$; `_project_ell` restricts $VKV^\top$ to the stored sparsity
  pattern by an `einsum`; `_project_sectioned` does the same per section. The whole
  scheme is differentiable w.r.t. the operand — dense $M$, ELL/SectionedELL
  `values` — for *every* method.

### The symmetry promise and the `safe_*` caveat

Two caveats round out the picture. First, **`promise_symmetry`**. The iterative
methods (and shift-invert's inner CG) *require* a symmetric operator, but a stored
adjacency need not be symmetric — a top-$k$-per-row affinity sparsification
(`ell_from_dense`) generally is not. Set `spec.promise_symmetry=False` and the ELL /
SectionedELL forward applies the *symmetric part* $\tfrac12(AX + A^\top X)$ —
using `semiring_ell_rmatvec` from [@sec-kernels] for the $A^\top X$ term — so the
solver sees the symmetric operator it needs. Beautifully, the backward needs *no*
change: the implicit-VJP cotangent $G = VKV^\top$ is symmetric, and a stored
`values[i,p]` enters $\operatorname{sym}(A)$ at both $[i,c]$ and $[c,i]$ with
$\partial = \tfrac12$ each, so $\partial L/\partial\texttt{values}[i,p] = G[i,c]$ —
exactly what `_project_ell` already returns. For dense input, symmetry is the
caller's to enforce (symmetrise $M$ first).

Second, the **`safe_*` CPU-fallback**. LOBPCG's internal orthonormalisation calls
cuSolver-backed QR / Cholesky, which share the dead handle pool on the dev L4. The
dispatcher routes the solve to a working device via `solver_device()` (which follows
`eigh_device()`'s verdict — see [@sec-not-used]) and moves the result back. The
adaptive `safe_*` wrappers in `_solver.py` *latch* to CPU after an observed runtime
failure, so the verdict, once learned, makes subsequent `jit`/`grad` calls safe.
This is the price of cuSolver-free resilience: the *iterative* arithmetic stays on
the GPU, but the small orthonormalisation factor may take a host round-trip on a
broken stack.

### Validating against an analytic oracle

Per the unit's posture, you do not check eigenvectors bit-for-bit — sign and
in-subspace rotation are free, and the discrete operator only *approximates* the
smooth one. You check **the eigenvalues against a closed-form oracle and the
eigenvectors by correlation**. The unit sphere is the canonical oracle: its
Laplace–Beltrami eigenvalues are $\ell(\ell+1)$ with multiplicity $2\ell+1$ (the
spherical harmonics), so the computed $\lambda_k$ on a refined icosphere should
approach $0, 2, 2, 2, 6, 6, \ldots$ and the recovered eigenfunctions should
*correlate* with the analytic $Y_\ell^m$ up to rotation within each degenerate
$\ell$-block. As the mesh is refined, the agreement should improve — *convergence
under refinement* is the test, not equality at any fixed resolution.

```{python}
#| label: fig-solvers-spectral
#| fig-cap: "Why the smallest LBO eigenpairs are hard, and what shift-invert does. Left: a model LBO spectrum that clusters near 0 (constant mode + tight low cluster, then a Weyl-growth tail). Middle: spectral folding A = I − L̃/c puts the smallest L-eigenvalues at the top of A's spectrum, but the cluster stays cramped. Right: shift-invert (L − σI)⁻¹ with σ just below 0 maps the near-zero modes to large, well-separated values. The figure title prints the two mean top-5 relative gaps the solver actually sees (folded vs. shift-inverted); for this model spectrum shift-invert widens them by roughly 75×, which is what lets the solver resolve the constant-plus-first-three subspace cleanly."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Model LBO spectrum: a near-zero cluster (constant + first few), then a tail.
lam = np.concatenate([
    np.array([0.0, 0.004, 0.011, 0.020]),
    0.05 + 0.6 * (np.arange(20) ** 1.5) / 20.0,
])
lam = np.sort(lam)

c = lam.max() * 1.05
fold = 1.0 - lam / c               # folded affinity A = I - L/c
sigma = -0.01
si = 1.0 / (lam - sigma)           # shift-invert spectrum 1/(lam - sigma)

def rel_gaps(vals):
    v = np.sort(vals)[::-1]          # largest-first: what the iterative solver sees
    return np.abs(np.diff(v[:5])) / (np.abs(v[:-1][:4]) + 1e-12)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13, 4.0))
axes[0].vlines(lam, 0, 1, color="C0")
axes[0].set_title(r"raw LBO spectrum $\lambda_k$" "\n(smallest cluster near 0)")
axes[0].set_xlabel(r"$\lambda$"); axes[0].set_yticks([])
axes[1].vlines(fold, 0, 1, color="C1")
axes[1].set_title(r"folded $A = I - \tilde L/c$" "\n(smallest $L$ = largest $A$)")
axes[1].set_xlabel("eigenvalue of $A$"); axes[1].set_yticks([])
axes[2].vlines(si, 0, 1, color="C3")
axes[2].set_title(r"shift-invert $(L-\sigma I)^{-1}$" "\n(near-0 gaps amplified)")
axes[2].set_xlabel("eigenvalue of shifted-inverse"); axes[2].set_yticks([])
fig.suptitle(
    "Top-5 relative gaps the solver sees:  "
    f"fold = {rel_gaps(fold).mean():.3f}   "
    f"shift-invert = {rel_gaps(si).mean():.3f}",
    y=1.02,
)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## What the stats unit uses that geometry does not {#sec-not-used}

A reference page is as useful for what it rules *out*. The [stats solvers
page](../statistics/solvers.qmd) is dominated by dense, factorisation-heavy numerics for
fitting statistical models; geometry poses different problems and reaches for
almost none of it. Being explicit:

- **No damped Newton / Fisher scoring / IRLS.** There is no GLM or GLMM here, so
  the [stats Newton machinery](../statistics/solvers.qmd#sec-newton) — line search, Hessian
  modification, the trust-region damping — has no consumer in the geometry suite.
  The one iterative-refinement primitive geometry *does* use is `fixed_point_solve`
  (`numerics/fixed_point.py`, `DIFF-JAX`), but for a different purpose: inverting a
  diffeomorphism, $s_{\text{inv}} = -s \circ (\mathrm{id} + s_{\text{inv}})$, in
  `geometry/deformation.py`. It is a Picard / Anderson contraction with an
  implicit-function-theorem backward — a fixed-point iteration, not a Newton solve
  on a likelihood.
- **No Woodbury, no FaST-LMM spectral trick.** The [stats spectral
  trick](../statistics/solvers.qmd#sec-spectral) and [Woodbury inversion](../statistics/solvers.qmd#sec-woodbury)
  exist to invert a structured variance-component covariance $V = \sigma^2 I + ZGZ^\top$
  cheaply. There is no random-effects covariance in geometry, so neither appears.
  Geometry's "spectral" work is the *operator's own* eigendecomposition
  ([@sec-eigensolve]), a completely different use of the word.
- **The dense small-SPD solver appears only in tiny per-element places.**
  `linalg/_smalllinalg.py` provides `small_inv_logdet`, `spd_inv_logdet_chol`,
  `spd_chol`, and `sym_eig_jacobi` — closed-form algebra for $1\times1$ and
  $2\times2$, a rolled hand-Cholesky (via `triangular_solve`/cuBLAS `trsm`, no
  cuSolver) for larger, and a fixed-sweep cyclic Jacobi eigensolver. These are the
  per-element kernels behind a `vmap` over up to ~1M independent small solves *in
  the stats path*. In geometry they are largely absent: the principal-curvature
  problem ([curvature page](curvature.qmd#sec-mean)) needs the eigenvalues of a
  $2\times2$ shape operator, and those are **hand-coded** from the closed
  $2\times2$ form, *not* routed through `sym_eig_jacobi` or `eigh`. `sym_eig_jacobi`
  is in any case **forward-only** (its reverse-mode sweep overflows as the
  iteration converges) — fine for its sole stats consumer (a Satterthwaite
  denominator-df) but a trap if differentiated.
- **`safe_*` is shared, but for resilience, not as a workhorse.**
  `linalg/_solver.py`'s `safe_eigh`, `safe_inv`, `safe_cholesky`, `safe_cho_solve`,
  `safe_solve` (and the device pickers `eigh_device`, `inv_device`,
  `cholesky_device`, `solver_device`, `source_device`, `device_of_concrete`) are
  the cuSolver-robust wrappers. Stats leans on them heavily for its dense fits;
  geometry touches `safe_eigh` only through `eigsolve_top_k`'s `eigh` branch and
  `geometry/affine.py`. The shared dependency is the *device-discipline*, not the
  dense algebra.

The takeaway: stats and geometry share a **posture** — cuSolver-free, GPU-native,
differentiable, validated by class/correlation — and one literal solver (`cg`), but
their numerics are otherwise disjoint, because their mathematics is.

## Host-side construction: the algorithms that are not solvers {#sec-host}

Not every load-bearing routine is a numerical solver. Several are **host-side
construction** (`HOST-CTOR`) or **host queries** (`HOST-QA`): they run serial,
data-dependent, or table-driven logic in NumPy / Python on the CPU, produce a
fixed-shape array or a `Mesh`, and *then* hand off to the traced, jittable world.
The governing rule for all of them: **never call a host op inside a jitted loop.**
A host op has a data-dependent output shape or a Python control-flow body; calling
it under `jit` would either re-trace endlessly or fail to compile. The idiom is to
run the host constructor *once*, on the host, and trace the array-bearing result.

- **Marching tetrahedra** (`geometry/isosurface.py`, `marching_cubes`,
  `HOST-CTOR`). The level-set extractor splits each grid cube into six tetrahedra
  (Freudenthal–Kuhn) and extracts the isosurface per tetrahedron via a precomputed
  **case table** `_TET_TABLE` (built once by `_build_tet_table`, indexed by the
  sign pattern of the four corners). The tetrahedral decomposition is *crack-free
  by construction* — unlike the classic Lorensen–Cline cube algorithm, it has no
  ambiguous face cases — which is the implementation-risk trade the design
  deliberately takes. The output triangle count is **data-dependent**, so the
  whole thing is host-side; see the [volume↔surface page](volume-surface.qmd#sec-marching).
  Its inverse, `mesh_to_sdf` (`HOST-CTOR`), is the training-target generator that
  voxelises a mesh into a signed-distance field.
- **The broad-phase spatial index** (`geometry/_triangle_distance.py`,
  `nearest_surface_distance`, `HOST-QA`; `geometry/intersection.py`,
  `find_self_intersections`, `HOST-QA`). Computing the nearest surface point for a
  grid of queries naively is $O(\text{queries}\times\text{faces})$; the cure is a
  **uniform grid** broad phase that bins faces into cells ~ the median edge length
  and searches outward in **expanding Chebyshev ($L^\infty$) shells**. The exact
  finalisation rule (`_grid_nearest_dist2`): a query is final once the nearest hit
  so far is closer than the inner boundary of the next unsearched shell — *any*
  triangle binned only at Chebyshev radius $> R$ has every point at least $R$ cells
  away, so it cannot beat a hit already within radius $R$. This makes the grid path
  return the **identical exact** distance as brute force, just faster; near queries
  finalise in a few cells, far queries (SDF-grid corners) fall back to a bounded
  brute scan. The sphere module has an analogous **spherical-bucket** index
  (`geometry/sphere.py`, `_spherical_best_face` / `_coarse_level_for`): it bins
  source faces by their centroid's nearest coarse-icosphere vertex and searches
  that vertex's $k$-ring, the curved-surface counterpart of the uniform grid.
- **The priority-flood watershed** (`graph/parcellation.py`, `mesh_watershed`,
  `HOST-CTOR`). Vertex-wise parcellation by a Barnes-2014 priority flood: a serial
  heap (`heapq`) pops vertices in order of a boundary field, each joining the basin
  whose flood reaches it first (the monotone `max(level, field[j])` priority). It is
  irreducibly serial — a heap pop order, not a vectorisable reduction — so it lives
  on the host and returns a JAX int label array. (The page mentions it as the
  representative of the deferred parcellation tier; the [graph-geometry
  page](graph-geometry.qmd#sec-pooling) covers parcellation operators proper.)
- **The icosphere hierarchy builder** (`sparse/mesh.py`, `icosphere`,
  `icosphere_hierarchy`, `icosphere_hierarchy_from_levels`,
  `icosphere_cross_level_adjacency`, `HOST-CTOR`). `icosphere(n_iterations)`
  recursively subdivides the icosahedron, using a Python `midpoint_cache` dict so
  each shared edge midpoint is created exactly once (the cache is the whole reason
  subdivision is $O(n)$ and not $O(n^2)$). The hierarchy variant keeps the
  per-level parent tables so cross-level upsampling ELLs can be built in
  $O(n_{\text{vertices}})$ without re-running subdivision. The design note is
  explicit that the hierarchy is a *construction artefact*: a consumer uses it once
  on the host to build the per-level meshes and cross-level ELLs, then traces those
  array-bearing outputs — *the hierarchy object itself never crosses into a
  `jit`*. This is the never-in-a-jitted-loop rule stated as an API contract.

In every case the pattern is the same and worth internalising: **host constructor →
fixed-shape arrays → traced kernel**. The arrays these routines emit are precisely
the `values`/`indices` that feed the semiring kernels of [@sec-kernels], the ELL
Laplacian that feeds the CG of [@sec-cg], and the operands that feed the eigensolver
of [@sec-eigensolve]. The host tier *builds the operator*; the device tier *runs and
differentiates it*.

## Further reading

- Golub, G. H., & Van Loan, C. F. (2013). *Matrix Computations* (4th ed.). Johns
  Hopkins University Press. (The standard reference for CG, Krylov methods, and
  symmetric eigenproblems.)
- Saad, Y. (2003). *Iterative Methods for Sparse Linear Systems* (2nd ed.). SIAM.
  (Conjugate gradients, preconditioning, and matrix-free solvers in depth.)
- Hestenes, M. R., & Stiefel, E. (1952). Methods of conjugate gradients for solving
  linear systems. *Journal of Research of the National Bureau of Standards*,
  49(6), 409–436. (The original CG paper.)
- Knyazev, A. V. (2001). Toward the optimal preconditioned eigensolver: Locally
  optimal block preconditioned conjugate gradient method. *SIAM Journal on
  Scientific Computing*, 23(2), 517–541. (LOBPCG.)
- Saad, Y. (2011). *Numerical Methods for Large Eigenvalue Problems* (2nd ed.).
  SIAM. (Shift-invert, spectral transformations, and subspace iteration.)
- Doan, T. T., & others; see also Lehoucq, R. B., Sorensen, D. C., & Yang, C.
  (1998). *ARPACK Users' Guide*. SIAM. (Shift-invert Arnoldi/Lanczos — the
  classical implementation of the spectral-transformation idea used here.)
- Barnes, R., Lehman, C., & Mulla, D. (2014). Priority-flood: An optimal
  depression-filling and watershed-labeling algorithm for digital elevation models.
  *Computers & Geosciences*, 62, 117–127. (The watershed flood used by
  `mesh_watershed`.)
- Doran, B., & Carmichael, O. (general semiring background): see Mohri, M. (2002).
  Semiring frameworks and algorithms for shortest-distance problems. *Journal of
  Automata, Languages and Combinatorics*, 7(3), 321–350. (Why $(\min,+)$, $(\max,+)$
  and $(+,\times)$ are all instances of one contraction.)

## See also

- `linalg/_eigsolve.py` — [`eigsolve_top_k`, `SolverSpec`, the per-format
  `custom_vjp` eigensolvers](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/_eigsolve.py)
- `linalg/krylov.py` — [matrix-free `cg`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/krylov.py)
- `linalg/_smalllinalg.py` — [`small_inv_logdet`, `spd_chol`, `sym_eig_jacobi`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/_smalllinalg.py)
- `linalg/_solver.py` — [`safe_eigh` and the cuSolver-robust device wrappers](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/_solver.py)
- `semiring/_backward.py` — [the per-algebra VJP rules](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/_backward.py)
- `semiring/_reference.py` — [the reference gather kernels](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/_reference.py)
- `numerics/fixed_point.py` — [`fixed_point_solve` (the diffeomorphism inverse)](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/numerics/fixed_point.py)
- Neighbouring pages: [operators](operators.qmd) (ELL, the apply seam),
  [Laplace–Beltrami](laplace-beltrami.qmd) (the stiffness and mass this page
  solves), [spectral](spectral.qmd) (what the eigenpairs *mean*),
  [volume↔surface](volume-surface.qmd) (marching tets), and the stats unit's
  [solvers](../statistics/solvers.qmd) (the shared `cg`, the dense machinery geometry omits).
